  - This violation of the parallel trends assumption is common.

### Approach 2: Sequential Ignorability (SI)

- Extend LDV model to the panel data setting.

- **Identification Assumption**:
  
  1. [Sequential Ignorability]{.highlight} [@robins1986new]:
     $$
     \{Y_{it}(1), Y_{it}(0)\} \ \perp \!\!\!\! \perp \ T_{it} \ \given \ \mathbf{X}_{it} = \mathbf{x} \quad \text{for any } \mathbf{x}
     $$

  2. [Positivity]{.highlight}:
     $$
     0 < \Pr(T_{it} = 1 \given \mathbf{X}_{it} = \mathbf{x}) < 1 \quad \text{for any } \mathbf{x}
     $$

- Importantly, $\mathbf{X}_{it}$ can include _any_ pre-treatment covariates, such as:
  
  - Past outcomes ($Y_{i, t-1}, Y_{i, t-2}, \ldots$).
  - Past treatments ($T_{i, t-1}, T_{i, t-2}, \ldots$).
  - Time fixed effects (dummy variables for time periods) but no unit fixed effects.

. . .

- **Advantage**: Allow for any feedback between the outcome and the treatment.

- **Disadvantage**: Does not allow for any unmeasured confounder.

### Identification and Estimation under SI

<br>

- **Identification**: Under the assumptions on the last slide
  $$
  \begin{align*}
  \tau(t) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} \{\E (Y_{it} \given T_{it} = 1, \mathbf{X}_{it} = \mathbf{x}) - \E (Y_{it} \given T_{it} = 0, \mathbf{X}_{it} = \mathbf{x})\} f(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x}
  = \E _{\mathbf{X}}\{\mu_1(\mathbf{X}_{it}) - \mu_0(\mathbf{X}_{it})\}
  \end{align*}
  $$

  where $\mu_t(\mathbf{x}) = \E (Y_{it} \given T_{it} = t, \mathbf{X}_{it} = \mathbf{x})$ for $t=0,1$.


- **Estimation**:
  - Essentially the same as "Regression in Observational Studies."
  - Choose a model for $\E (Y_{it} \given T_{it} = t, \mathbf{X}_{it} = \mathbf{x})$.

- [Example]{.highlight}: Linear Model with OLS
  $$
  \E (Y_{it} \given T_{it} = t, \mathbf{X}_{it} = \mathbf{x}) = \alpha + \tau T_{it} + \mathbf{X}_i^\prime \gamma
  $$

  - Cluster standard errors at the unit level.

### Practical Consideration + Extensions

- **TWFE estimator**:
  - ✅ Can account for additive unmeasured time-invariant confounder.
  - ❌ No simple non-parametric DiD estimators to check the TWFE.
  - ❌ Parallel trends assumption is hard to assess.

- **Methodology based on the sequential ignorability**:
  - ✅ We can allow for any feedback between outcome and treatment.
  - ❌ Assume no unmeasured confounding.

. . .

- Recent Methodologies to use the parallel trends assumption:
  - @imai2023matching and [R]{.proglang} package `PanelMatch`.
  - @dechaisemartin2020two and [R]{.proglang} package `DIDmultiplegtDYN`.
  - @chiu2023and and [R]{.proglang} package `fect`.

- Many Methodologies to use the sequential ignorability:
  - @robins2000marginal
  - @blackwell2018make

# Synthetic Control Method

### From Many Units to One

<br><br>

- So far: DiD and panel methods assume **many treated units** $\rightsquigarrow$ can estimate group means and differences.

. . .

- **What if there is only one treated unit?** (e.g., a single state passes a policy)

  - Cannot estimate $\E[Y_{it}(0) \given G_i = 1]$ from data $\rightsquigarrow$ need to **construct** a counterfactual from donor units.

### Setup

<br>

- Suppose that we observe $J+1$ units in periods $1, ..., T_0, ..., T$.
- Treatment kicks in for unit 1 at time $T_0$.
- The unit remains exposed to the treatment thereafter.
- The other $J$ units do not receive the treatment throughout the study period.

- **Treatment Variable**:

  - $T_{1t} = 1$ if $t \geq T_0$ and $T_{1t} = 0$ if $t < T_0$.
  - $T_{it} = 0$ for all other units $i \in \{2, \ldots, J+1\}$ and $t \in \{1, \ldots, T\}$.

- **Potential outcome**: $Y_{it}(d)$.
- **Causal Estimand**: Treatment effect on unit $1$ at time $T_0+1, \ldots, T$ (special case of _ATT_):
  $$
  \tau_{1t} \ \equiv \ Y_{1t}(1) - Y_{1t}(0) = Y_{1t} - \textcolor{#cc241d}{Y_{1t}(0)} \quad \text{for} \quad t > T_{0}.
  $$

### Example: California's Proposition 99

<br><br>

- @abadie2010synthetic: Comprehensive tobacco control legislation in California (1988)

  - Increased cigarette tax by 25 cents/pack.
  - Earmarked tax revenues to health and anti-smoking budgets.
  - Funded anti-smoking media campaigns.
  - Spurred clean-air ordinances throughout the state.
  - Produced more than \$100 million per year in anti-tobacco projects.

. . .

- Donor Pool

  - Other states that subsequently passed control programs are excluded from the donor pool of controls (AK, AZ, FL, HA, MA, MD, MI, NJ, NY, OR, WA, DC).


### Cigarette Consumption: CA & the Rest of the U.S.

![](../_images/ca1.png){width="75%" fig-align="center"}

## Identification

### DiD vs Synthetic Control

<br>

- [DiD]{.highlight}: Focus on _ATT_ and assume parallel trends.

  $$
  Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \gamma_t + \tau T_{it} + \mathbf{X}_{it}^\prime \beta + \varepsilon_{it}, \quad \text{with } \E [\varepsilon_{it} \given T_{it}, \mathbf{X}_{it}] = 0.
  $$

- This implies untreated trend is a [TWFE process]{.highlight} $Y_{jt}(0) = \alpha_i + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{it}.$

. . .

- [Synthetic Control (SCM)]{.highlight}: Assume untreated trend is an [interactive FE (factor) model]{.highlight}:

  $$
  Y_{it}(0) = \gamma_t + \lambda_t^\prime \pmb{\mu_i} + \beta_t^\prime \mathbf{X}_i + \varepsilon_{it},
  $$

  where
    
    - $\lambda_t = (\lambda_{t1}, \ldots, \lambda_{tR})'$ are time shocks (factors) with $T_0 > R$.
    - $\mu_i = (\mu_{i1}, \ldots, \mu_{iR})'$ are unit-specific loadings.
    - $\mathbf{X}_i$ should not be post-treatment!

. . .

- [Note]{.note}: If $\lambda_t = \lambda$, then this is TWFE.

### Identification

<br>

- [Idea]{.note}: Take a weighted sum of $J$ comparison (donor) units.

- Weight vector $w = (w_j)_{j=2}^{J+1}$ where $w_j \geq 0$ and $\sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j = 1$.

- Weighted sums of control observations is then:

  $$
  \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j Y_{jt} 
  = \gamma_t + \beta_t^\prime \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j \mathbf{X}_j + \lambda_t^\prime \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j \pmb{\mu}_j + \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j \varepsilon_{jt}
  $$

. . .

:::{.callout-important icon="false" title="SCM Balancing Weights"}
Let $w^\ast$ be the weights that satisfy $\sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^\ast \pmb{\mu}_j = \pmb{\mu}_1$ and $\sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^\ast \mathbf{X}_j = \mathbf{X}_1$.
:::

- These are called [balancing weights]{.highlight} cause the $X_j$ term to cancel out when we take $Y_{1t}(0) - \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^\ast Y_{jt}$ for $t \leq T_{0}$ $\rightsquigarrow$ use them to approximate $Y_{1t}(0)$ for $t > T_{0}$.

## Estimation and Inference

### Estimation

- Need to have $\pmb{\mu}_j$ and estimate synthetic weights $\mathbf{W} = (w_2,\dots, w_{J+1})^\prime$ (representing synth control).
  
  - **Solution**: Use $(Y_{j,1},\dots,Y_{j,T_0})$ for $\pmb{\mu}_j$ and solve for $\mathbf{W}$.

. . .


- [Optimisation problem]{.highlight}: Choose synthetic weights $\mathbf{W}^\ast$ such that 

  - $w_i \geq 0$ for $i \in \{2,...,J+1\}$ and $\sum_{i=2}^{J+1} w_i = 1$,
  - $\forall t < T_0$ (pre-treatment periods) $\mathbf{W}^\ast$ minimizes
  
  $$
  | X_{1t} - \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w^\ast_j X_{jt}|, \quad | Y_{11} - \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w^\ast_j Y_{j1} |.
  $$

  - (what is the problem here?)

. . .

- Then an _approximately_ unbiased estimator of $\tau_{1t}$ is:
  $$
  \widehat \tau_{1t} \ = \ Y_{1t} - \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w^*_j Y_{jt} \quad \text{for} \quad t \in \{T_0+1,\dots ,T\}.
  $$


### Constructing Synthetic Weights

<br>

- Let $\mathbf{V}_1 = (X_{11}, ..., X_{1R}, Y_{11}, ..., Y_{1T_0})^\prime$ be a $p$-dimensional vector of pre-intervention characteristics where $p = R + T_0$.

- $\mathbf{V}_0$ is a $(p \times J)$ matrix of the same variables for the control units.

. . .

- $\mathbf{W}$ is chosen to minimize $\|\mathbf{V}_1 - \mathbf{V}_0 \mathbf{W}\|$, subject to constraints:
  - $w_i \geq 0$ for $i \in \{2,...,J+1\}$ and $\sum_{i=2}^{J+1} w_i = 1$.

- Use $\|\mathbf{V}_1 - \mathbf{V}_0 \mathbf{W}\| = \sqrt{(\mathbf{V}_1 - \mathbf{V}_0 \mathbf{W})^\prime \Sigma (\mathbf{V}_1 - \mathbf{V}_0 \mathbf{W})}$, where $\Sigma$ is some $(p \times p)$ symmetric and positive semidefinite matrix.

. . .

- @abadie2010synthetic choose $\Sigma$ to minimize the MSPE for the pre-treatment outcomes for the treated unit:
  $$
  MSPE \ = \ \frac{1}{T_0}\sum_{t=1}^{T_0}\left(Y_{1t} - \sum_{j=2}^{J+1}w_j^\ast Y_{jt}\right)^2.
  $$

### DiD vs Synthetic Control

<br><br><br>

- Synthetic Control and DiD are both [counterfactual approximation methods]{.highlight}:

. . .

- [DiD]{.highlight}: uniform weights and intercept adjustment  
  $$
  \widehat{Y}_{1t}(0) = \alpha_{1} + \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} \frac{1}{J} Y_{jt}
  $$

- [SCM]{.highlight}: variable, convex weights with no intercept  
  $$
  \widehat{Y}_{1t}(0) = \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j Y_{jt}
  $$

### Cigarette Consumption: CA & the Rest of the U.S.

![](../_images/ca1.png){width="75%" fig-align="center"}


### Cigarette Consumption: CA and Synthetic CA

![](../_images/ca2.png){width="75%" fig-align="center"}


### Synthetic Weights

![](../_images/ca9.png){width="60%" fig-align="center"}


### Predictor Means: Actual vs. Synthetic California

<br><br>


| Variables                     | Real          | Synthetic     | Average of 38 control states |
|-------------------------------|:-------------:|:-------------:|:-------------:|
| Ln(GDP per capita)            | 10.08  | 9.86      | 9.86                         |
| Percent aged 15-24            | 17.40  | 17.40     | 17.29                        |
| Retail price                  | 89.42  | 89.41     | 87.27                        |
| Beer consumption per capita   | 24.28  | 24.20     | 23.75                        |
| Cigarette sales per capita 1988 | 90.10 | 91.62     | 114.20                       |
| Cigarette sales per capita 1980 | 120.20 | 120.43   | 136.58                       |
| Cigarette sales per capita 1975 | 127.10 | 126.99   | 132.81                       |



### Smoking Gap Between CA and Synthetic CA

![](../_images/ca3.png){width="75%" fig-align="center"}



### Inference

- **Approach 1**: [Permutation (Fisher) Inference]{.highlight} [@abadie2010synthetic]

  - [Idea]{.note}: Construct "placebo" estimates using SCM for post-treatment donor observations.

  - **Procedure**:

    - Iteratively apply the synthetic method to each control unit and obtain a distribution of "placebo effects."
  
    - Compare the gap for California to the distribution of the placebo gaps.

. . .
 
- **Approach 2**: ["Conformal inference"]{.highlight} [@chernozhukov2021exact]

  
  - [Idea]{.note}: Look at differences in pre- and post-treatment prediction residuals. 
  
  - **Procedure**:
    
    - Apply the synthetic control method to the each pre and post treatment period and calculate $u_{t} = Y_{1t} - \widehat{Y}_{1t} (0)$.
  
    - Under sharp null $\forall t: \: Y_{1t} (0) = Y_{1t} (1) = Y_{1t}$ $\rightsquigarrow$ $u_{t}$ at $t > T_{0}$ comes from the same distribution as $t <= T_{0}$.

### Permutation Test: CA vs 38 Control States

![](../_images/ca4.png){width="75%" fig-align="center"}

- Looks good, but what is wrong with **bad** placebos pre-Proposition 99?

### Permutation Test: CA vs 34 Control States {data-visibility="uncounted"}

![](../_images/ca5.png){width="75%" fig-align="center"}

- Discard states with pre-Proposition 99 MSPE **twenty** times higher than California’s

### Permutation Test: CA vs 29 Control States {data-visibility="uncounted"}

![](../_images/ca6.png){width="75%" fig-align="center"}

- Discard states with pre-Proposition 99 MSPE **five** times higher than California’s

### Permutation Test: CA vs 19 Control States {data-visibility="uncounted"}

![](../_images/ca7.png){width="75%" fig-align="center"}

- Discard states with pre-Proposition 99 MSPE **two** times higher than California’s

### Ratio Post-Prop. 99 MSPE to Pre-Prop. 99 MSPE

![](../_images/ca8.png){width="75%" fig-align="center"}

- [Permutation distribution]{.highlight} California is clear outlier!

### Summary and Extensions

<br>

- Synthetic control method is like a _"quantitative case study"_ [@abadie2010synthetic]: **only one or few treated units needed, but long time series**.

- In practice you need to look at the resulting weights: sometimes few controls receive any weight (_"corner solution" problem_).

- See @abadie2021using and replication materials for @abadie2015comparative for implementation.

. . .

- Many scholars are extending it now:  
  
  1. **Extension to the SCM design:**  
    
      - @xu2017generalized: Generalized SCM and [R]{.proglang} package `fect`.
      - @ben2022synthetic: Augmented SCM and [R]{.proglang} package `augsynth`.  
      - @arkhangelsky2021synthetic: DiD + Synthetic Controls and [R]{.proglang} package `synthdid`.

  2. **Exploring assumptions behind SCM**: @doudchenko2016balancing, @bottmer2024design

## Summary

### Method Selection Guide

```{tikz}
%| label: method-selection-tree
%| fig-align: center
%| fig-width: 7
%| echo: false

\usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric, backgrounds}

\definecolor{gruvbg}{HTML}{f0f1eb}
\definecolor{gruvfg}{HTML}{111111}
\definecolor{gruvred}{HTML}{cc241d}
\definecolor{gruvgreen}{HTML}{98971a}
\definecolor{gruvblue}{HTML}{458588}
\definecolor{gruvpurple}{HTML}{b16286}
\definecolor{gruvorange}{HTML}{d65d0e}
\definecolor{gruvaqua}{HTML}{689d6a}

\begin{tikzpicture}[
  background rectangle/.style={fill=gruvbg},
  show background rectangle,
  >=Stealth,
  node distance=1.2cm and 2.2cm,
  question/.style={
    diamond,
    draw=gruvfg,
    fill=gruvbg,
    text=gruvfg,
    text width=2.8cm,
    align=center,
    inner sep=2pt,
    font=\small\bfseries
  },
  method/.style={
    rectangle,
    rounded corners=4pt,
    draw=#1,
    fill=#1!15,
    text=gruvfg,
    text width=2.8cm,
    align=center,
    minimum height=0.8cm,
    font=\small\bfseries
  },
  method/.default=gruvblue,
  edge label/.style={
    font=\footnotesize,
    text=gruvfg,
    fill=gruvbg,
    inner sep=1pt
  },
  arrow/.style={->, thick, gruvfg}
]

% Questions
\node[question] (q1) {How many\\treated units?};
\node[question, below right=1.5cm and 3cm of q1] (q2) {Treatment\\absorbing?};
\node[question, below left=1.5cm and 1.5cm of q2] (q3) {Single\\treatment\\time?};
\node[question, below right=1.5cm and 1.5cm of q3] (q4) {Long\\pre-trend?};

% Methods
\node[method=gruvaqua, below left=1.5cm and 2cm of q1] (scm) {Classic SCM};
\node[method=gruvblue, below left=1.2cm and 0.8cm of q3] (did) {Classic\\DiD/TWFE};
\node[method=gruvpurple, below left=1.2cm and 0.8cm of q4] (sa) {HTE-robust\\SA estimators};
\node[method=gruvorange, below right=1.2cm and 0.8cm of q4] (ife) {IFE\\Imputation};
\node[method=gruvred, below right=1.5cm and 1cm of q2] (si) {Sequential\\Ignorability};

% Arrows
\draw[arrow] (q1) -- node[edge label, above left] {One} (scm);
\draw[arrow] (q1) -- node[edge label, above right] {Many} (q2);
\draw[arrow] (q2) -- node[edge label, above left] {Yes} (q3);
\draw[arrow] (q2) -- node[edge label, above right] {No (on/off)} (si);
\draw[arrow] (q3) -- node[edge label, left] {Yes} (did);
\draw[arrow] (q3) -- node[edge label, right] {No (staggered)} (q4);
\draw[arrow] (q4) -- node[edge label, left] {No} (sa);
\draw[arrow] (q4) -- node[edge label, right] {Yes} (ife);

\end{tikzpicture}
```

### What to use?

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}

- [Classic DiD/TWFE]{.highlight}

  :::small-font
  - Single treatment cohort.
  - Parallel trends (potentially conditionally).
  - Control trends don't need to overlap treated trend.
  - Short pretrend data.
  :::

- [Classic SCM]{.highlight}

  :::small-font
  - Single treatment unit.
  - Unweighted trends not necessarily parallel.
  - Control trends overlap treated trend.
  - Long pretrend data.
  :::

- [Sequential Ignorability]{.highlight}

  :::small-font
  - General treatment pattern (on/off).
  - No unmeasured confounding (given history).
  - Allows outcome-treatment feedback.
  :::

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

- [HTE-robust SA Estimators]{.highlight}

  :::small-font
  - Multiple cohorts, staggered adoption (absorbing).
  - Parallel trends (potentially conditionally).
  - Control trends don't overlap treated trend.
  - Short pretrend data.
  :::

- [Interactive FE Imputation Methods]{.highlight}

  :::small-font
  - Multiple treated units, possibly at different times.
  - Control trends overlap treated trends.
  - Long pretrend data.
  :::

:::
:::


# Appendix {visibility="uncounted"}



### References {visibility="uncounted"}
